candidate_problems.text: Can the genealogical claim be turned into a substantive limitation theorem rather than a historical observation? In other words, is there a principled argument that any framework individuated purely by causal role, control, representation, and prediction will systematically underdescribe or omit phenomenal consciousness, or is the conjecture only noting a contingent historical bias in current information-processing concepts?

What would a successor concept to 'information processing' have to add, formally and ontologically, to make phenomenal consciousness intelligible without collapsing back into functionalism or dualism? This opens the problem of specifying whether the needed revision concerns richer notions of information, new primitives about subjectivity, different explanatory standards, or an entirely different architecture of explanation.

How can we non-circularly distinguish the aspects of consciousness that are merely enabled by information processing (access, report, integration, self-modeling) from those that are constitutive of phenomenal experience itself? Without such a criterion, the asymmetry claim remains underdetermined, because any failure of current models could be blamed either on incomplete functional description or on a genuine explanatory remainder.
